---
title: "EMS_mutations"
output: html_document
---



```{r}
#to split fasta files into separate fasta for each chromosome, run this in linux terminal
#copied from https://sites.psu.edu/biomonika/2017/03/09/split-large-fasta-file-into-multiple-fasta-files-on-any-computer/
# csplit PvulgarisLaborOvalle_670_v1.0.fa /\>Chr.*/ {*} 
# every sequence is now in one file named xxx something
# for a in x*; do echo $a; mv $a $(head -1 $a).txt; done; 
#we just renamed each file by using first line of each file

#this is not absolutely necessary but it will stop the program from using up too much ram

library(stringr)
library(Biostrings)
#read text file containing genome data of the form AGTTCG... into temporary object
#currently set up to read (11th chromosome of) genome data of common bean
chrom_vector<-readLines(">Chr11.txt")
#remove lines that are headers for fasta files by eliminating lines with ">"
chrom_vector<- grep(">", chrom_vector, value = TRUE, invert = TRUE)
#test is transformed into list, called, with each nucleotide in file
chrom_vector<-strsplit(x=chrom_vector, "")
#overall mutation rate is set for SNPs. mutagens favor some transitions and these biases are set later
mut_rate<-.0001
#chrom_list is transformed into a character vector
chrom_vector<-unlist(chrom_vector)
#run garbage collection
gc()
#length of chromosome is calculated and stored in variable called chrom_len
chrom_len<-length(chrom_vector)
chrom_len
#the overall mutation rate and length of chromosome is used to find the total number of SNPs
num_of_muts<-mut_rate*chrom_len
#run garbage collection
gc()
#each transition type is done in sequence starting with A to T transition

#bias of A to T transition. 0.5 would mean that half of SNPs are of this type
portion_of_A_to_T<-0.1
#amount of SNPs that will be of the A to T type. rounded to nearest whole number
size_A_to_T<-round(num_of_muts*portion_of_A_to_T)
size_A_to_T
#locations for the SNPs are decided (pseudo)randomly using sample function in base R
mut_locations_A_to_T<-sample(x=chrom_len, size=size_A_to_T, replace=FALSE)

# location of each SNP is checked if it has an A nucleotide and, if so, is transitioned to T
#if the location does not have an A, a new location is randomly chosen and added to the end of the location list
#size_A_to_T variable is increased by 1 if mut_locations_A_to_T is added so new locations are used
#this strategy is used instead of going through each nucleotide and giving it a chance to transition
#the chosen strategy should be more efficient since not every nucleotide needs to be read.
#especially since very few nucleotides will be changed and there will be millions of unmodified nucleotides
for (i in 1:size_A_to_T) {
  latest_mut_location<-mut_locations_A_to_T[i]
  if (chrom_vector[latest_mut_location] == 'A') {
   chrom_vector[latest_mut_location] <- 'T'
   
  }
  else {
    append(mut_locations_A_to_T, sample(x=chrom_len, size=1))
    size_A_to_T<-size_A_to_T+1
  }
 
}
gc()
#same process but for C to G
portion_of_C_to_G<-0.4

size_C_to_G<-round(num_of_muts*portion_of_C_to_G)


mut_locations_C_to_G<-sample(x=chrom_len, size=size_C_to_G, replace=FALSE)
for (i in 1:size_C_to_G) {
  latest_mut_location<-mut_locations_C_to_G[i]
  if (chrom_vector[latest_mut_location] == 'C') {
   chrom_vector[latest_mut_location] <- 'G'
   
  }
  else {
    append(mut_locations_C_to_G, sample(x=chrom_len, size=1))
    size_C_to_G<-size_C_to_G+1
  }
 
}
gc()
#same process but for G to T
portion_of_G_to_T<-0.2

size_G_to_T<-round(num_of_muts*portion_of_G_to_T)


mut_locations_G_to_T<-sample(x=chrom_len, size=size_G_to_T, replace=FALSE)
for (i in 1:size_G_to_T) {
  latest_mut_location<-mut_locations_G_to_T[i]
  if (chrom_vector[latest_mut_location] == 'G') {
   chrom_vector[latest_mut_location] <- 'T'
   
  }
  else {
    append(mut_locations_G_to_T, sample(x=chrom_len, size=1))
    size_G_to_T<-size_G_to_T+1
  }
 
}

#same process but for T to C
portion_of_T_to_C<-0.3

size_T_to_C<-round(num_of_muts*portion_of_T_to_C)


mut_locations_T_to_C<-sample(x=chrom_len, size=size_T_to_C, replace=FALSE)
for (i in 1:size_T_to_C) {
  latest_mut_location<-mut_locations_T_to_C[i]
  if (chrom_vector[latest_mut_location] == 'T') {
   chrom_vector[latest_mut_location] <- 'C'
   
  }
  else {
    append(mut_locations_T_to_C, sample(x=chrom_len, size=1))
    size_T_to_C<-size_T_to_C+1
  }
 
}
gc()

#now we do insertion mutations
#assign number of insertions per chromosome (this could be modified to be randomized)
number_of_insertions<-50
#now determine locations for these insertions
insertion_locations<-sample(x=chrom_len, size=number_of_insertions, replace=FALSE)
insertion_locations<-sort(insertion_locations, decreasing = TRUE)
#set max length for insertions. min length is 1
insertion_max<-4
#create vector to sample from to create sequence for insertions
nucleotides<-c("A", "T", "G", "C")
 
for (i in 1:number_of_insertions) {
  latest_insertion_size<-sample(insertion_max, size=1)
  latest_insertion<-sample(nucleotides, size=latest_insertion_size)
  chrom_vector<-append(chrom_vector, values=latest_insertion, after=insertion_locations[i])
}
gc()
#now we do deletion mutations
#assign number of deletions per chromosome(this could be modified to be randomized)
number_of_deletions<-60
#now determine locations for these deletions
deletion_locations<-sample(x=chrom_len, size=number_of_deletions, replace=FALSE)
deletions_locations<-sort(deletion_locations, decreasing = TRUE)
#set max length for deletions. min is 1
deletion_max<-4
for (i in 1:number_of_deletions) {
  latest_deletion_size<-sample(deletion_max, size=1)
  chrom_vector[-((deletions_locations[i]):(deletions_locations[i]+latest_deletion_size))]
}
gc()
#create inversion mutations
#choose number of inversions
number_of_inversions<-50
#choose max inversion length (in base pairs)
max_inversion_length<-10
#choose avergae inversion length (in base pairs)
avg_inversion_length<-5
#sample from normal distribution

#generate locations for inversions
inversion_locations<-sample(x=chrom_len, size=number_of_inversions, replace = FALSE)
inversion_locations<-sort(inversion_locations, decreasing = TRUE)

for (i in 1:number_of_inversions) {
  #choose length of the inversion for current iteration
  latest_inversion_len<-sample(max_inversion_length, replace= FALSE, rnorm(n=1, mean = avg_inversion_length, sd = 1))
  #extract sequence using length and latest location
  sequence_for_inversion<-chrom_vector[inversion_locations[i]:inversion_locations[i]+latest_inversion_len]
  #invert segment
  inverted_segment<-rev(sequence_for_inversion)
  #drop non-inverted segment from original vector using negative indexing
  chrom_vector<-chrom_vector[-c(inversion_locations[i]:inversion_locations[i]+latest_inversion_len)]
  #now add inverted version of section to the original vector
  chrom_vector<-append(chrom_vector, values=inverted_segment, after = inversion_locations[i])
}
#preview chrom_vector
print("chrom")
print(chrom_vector)
#check if chosen gene has appeared in this instance

#load chosen gene
desired_mutation<-readLines("BAR_gene.txt")
#convert desired mutation to DNAstring container from biostrings package
desired_mutation<-paste0(desired_mutation, collapse = "")
desired_mutation<-DNAString(desired_mutation)
#do same for chrom_vector
chrom_vector<-paste0(chrom_vector, collapse = "")
chrom_DNAstring<-DNAString(chrom_vector)
#now we can use Biostrings functions to compare the sequences
matchPattern(desired_mutation, chrom_DNAstring, fixed = TRUE)

```
